title: Generics should be preferred when appropriate
description: Generics should be used when appropriateAnalyze your codeintentionality-clearmaintainabilityCode SmellWhy is this an issue?When a reference parameter (keywordref) is used, the passed argument type must exactly match the reference parameter type. This means that to be able to pass a derived type, it must be cast and assigned to a variable of the proper type. Use of generic methods eliminates that cumbersome down casting and should therefore be preferred.This rule raises an issue when a method contains arefparameter of typeSystem.Object.Noncompliant code exampleusing System; namespace MyLibrary { public class Foo { public void Bar(ref object o1, ref object o2) // Noncompliant { } } }Compliant solutionusing System; namespace MyLibrary { public class Foo { public void Bar<T>(ref T ref1, ref T
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant: public void DoSomething(ref object param) // Noncompliant
compliant: public void DoSomething<T>(ref T param) // Compliant

logic.detection_conditions: Detects methods with ref object parameters that should use generics instead
logic.triggering_conditions: Triggers when: method has a ref parameter of type object (ref object paramName)
logic.exclusion_conditions: Excludes: out parameters, methods already using generics, comments, generated code
never_trigger_on: Methods already using generic type parameters
logic.reasoning: This rule detects methods using 'ref object' parameters, which should be replaced with generic type parameters for better type safety and to avoid casting. The pattern 'ref object' is problematic because it forces callers to cast derived types to object before passing them. Using generics (ref T) eliminates this need. The regex pattern looks for 'ref' keyword followed by 'object' type and a parameter name. This is a straightforward pattern match as the issue is syntactic - any occurrence of 'ref object' in a parameter list should be flagged.

should_trigger: public void Bar(ref object o1, ref object o2)
should_not_trigger: public void Bar<T>(ref T ref1, ref T ref2)
